title: Vincetoxicum wilfordii
intro: Vincetoxicum wilfordii, also known as the Korean Iscador, is a plant species belonging to the Apocynaceae family. It has been traditionally used in Korean medicine to treat various health conditions, including cancer, due to its potential health benefits, which include anti-inflammatory, anti-oxidative, and anti-cancer properties. The bioactive constituents of Vincetoxicum wilfordii include vincetoxicine, vincetoxin, and vincaminoside, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with this plant include extracts, teas, and tablets, which are often used to support the treatment of cancer and other diseases, although more research is needed to fully understand its effects.
taxonomy: Vincetoxicum wilfordii is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Apocynaceae. It is a type of milkweed vine native to Korea and Japan. This species is also known as the Korean swallowwort or wilford's swallowwort. Vincetoxicum wilfordii is a perennial climber that grows in woodland edges and shaded areas. As a member of the Asclepiadaceae subfamily (now included in the Apocynaceae family), it is closely related to other milkweed species.

The plant is believed to have potential health benefits, including reducing inflammation, improving cardiovascular health, and boosting the immune system. It is also used to treat various conditions, such as fever, headache, and digestive issues. side_effects: Vincetoxicum wilfordii is a plant that has been used in traditional medicine, but its use may be associated with side effects such as nausea, vomiting, and dizziness. These side effects are often mild and temporary, but they can be severe in some cases, particularly when the plant is used in high doses or for extended periods. Some people may also experience allergic reactions, such as skin rashes or itching, after coming into contact with Vincetoxicum wilfordii.
